---
title: "Research"
---

::: {.lead-block}
### Variability and error in a wiring diagram
Genetically identical animals, raised alike, still don't wire identically. I measure
how much brains differ from one another, and treat the mismatches — neurons that
wander, double, or go missing — as evidence about how development can fail and how
robust the finished circuit really is.
:::

::: {.lead-block}
### Where a memory physically sits
With single-cell transcriptomics I track how training reshapes gene expression in
identified neurons. Massed and spaced learning, which produce short- and long-lasting
memory, share no differentially expressed genes at all in mushroom-body output
neurons — a hint that the molecular account of memory is not simply a slower version
of the synaptic one.
:::

::: {.lead-block}
### From genome to circuit
I make targeted genetic changes in single, identified neurons and measure how the
wiring responds, connecting what the genome specifies to what the connectome becomes.
:::
